note: Benefit-side / pro-immigration generative lenses — the mirror of S_restrictionist_steelman_full (14 gens). Built 2026-06-23 to fix the bank's structural asymmetry (rich on fiscal-cost lenses, thin on benefit-side). Each surfaces a mechanism by which immigration is net-positive that the standing cost ledger omits. source_rel_paths here are ACQUISITION TARGETS (papers not yet in corpus) — this cluster doubles as the benefit-side shopping list to balance the net-negative data frontier.

generators.name: Level-not-mean / additive-output gate
generators.prompt: Whenever a claim infers harm from 'immigrants lower the average education / wage / GDP-per-capita', block it until restated in LEVELS: did total output rise, and is any specific incumbent mechanically poorer, by what channel? A falling mean with rising total and no named incumbent loss is a composition artifact, not a welfare loss.
generators.retrodiction: Would have caught that 'the average American is more educated than the average LATAM entrant, therefore harm' confuses the mean with welfare. Output is additive; adding a $35k worker to a $70k-mean economy lowers the mean while raising total output and making no incumbent mechanically poorer. Reconciles rising aggregate GDP with falling GDP-per-capita-of-the-average.
generators.negative_space: Standing frame treats national average human capital as a welfare target that below-average entrants 'dilute'.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: A lower average implies some incumbent is worse off
The economy is a fixed pie / a GPA
generators.theory_tested: Total PERWT-weighted earnings in ipums_usa_borjas_panel should rise 1980→2023 even as the share-weighted mean education falls; per-capita-of-the-average is not the binding welfare quantity.

generators.name: Place premium / migrant-welfare ledger (whose welfare counts)
generators.prompt: For any 'is immigration good' verdict, name the welfare boundary explicitly: incumbent-natives-only, all-residents-incl-immigrants, or global. Then add the migrant's own gain — the place premium, the multiple a observably-identical worker earns in the US vs origin — as a labelled ledger row. A verdict that zeroes it must say so.
generators.retrodiction: Would have surfaced that every NAS / Borjas fiscal ledger is incumbent-only and silently sets the LARGEST welfare term to zero: the 2–15× place premium on the migrant's own earnings (Clemens-Montenegro-Pritchett). 'Net negative' on the incumbent-fiscal ledger is consistent with hugely net-positive on the resident or global ledger.
generators.negative_space: Cost ledgers count only the incumbent taxpayer's balance; the immigrant's own welfare gain is structurally invisible.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: Migrant welfare gain is out of scope for the policy question
generators.theory_tested: Border-selection earnings (G-LIF-G04) should show a discrete earnings jump at migration that dwarfs the lifetime fiscal NPV magnitude for the same cell.

generators.name: Consumer-surplus / non-traded price channel
generators.prompt: For low-skill labor inflows, add the native real-income gain from lower prices of immigrant-intensive non-traded services (childcare, food prep, construction, housekeeping, gardening). Quantify via the immigrant-share elasticity of local service prices. This benefit sits outside BOTH the fiscal ledger and the wage-competition ledger.
generators.retrodiction: Would have flagged that low-skill immigration's downward effect on non-traded service prices (Cortes 2008 JPE) is a real-wage gain to native consumers, absent from the fiscal NPV and from the Borjas wage ledger — natives who don't compete with immigrants gain as consumers.
generators.negative_space: Cost ledgers count fiscal + wage competition; the consumer-price benefit channel is unrepresented entirely.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: Immigrants affect natives only via taxes and wage competition
generators.theory_tested: PUMA/state immigrant share should correlate negatively with a local immigrant-intensive service price index (not yet in the warehouse — acquisition + build target).

generators.name: Entrepreneurship / labor-demand offset to the supply shock
generators.prompt: Before treating immigrants as pure labor SUPPLY (substitutes in a fixed job pool), net out the labor DEMAND they create: firm-formation and job-creation rates. Add immigrant founding share as a demand row that partly offsets the substitution in any supply-shock claim (e.g. G-LIF-B02).
generators.retrodiction: Would have caught that the supply-shock framing is one-sided: immigrants found firms at above-population rates and are net job CREATORS, not just takers (Azoulay-Jones-Kim-Miranda 2022) — the lump-of-labor assumption behind 'they take jobs' is the unnamed premise.
generators.negative_space: Supply-shock lenses count immigrants as competitors in the labor pool; their firm-formation / labor-demand contribution is uncounted.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: Immigrants are labor supply only, not labor demand
generators.theory_tested: Self-employment / business-ownership rate by NATIVITY (needs CLASSWKR added to the IPUMS extract) should be ≥ native rate for several origin cells.

generators.name: PAYG demographic dividend / SS–Medicare solvency
generators.prompt: For payroll-funded PAYG programs (OASDI, Medicare HI), credit immigrants' younger age structure: they pay in for decades before claiming, improving the dependency ratio and the trust-fund actuarial balance. Add the SSA actuary's immigration sensitivity (higher net immigration narrows the OASDI deficit) as a federal benefit row; for unauthorized workers add the SSN-mismatch contributions they fund but cannot claim.
generators.retrodiction: Would have surfaced that the static fiscal 'cost' of low-skill immigrants understates federal payroll contributions: the SS Trustees explicitly model higher immigration as IMPROVING OASDI solvency, and the Earnings Suspense File holds large unauthorized contributions never claimed back.
generators.negative_space: Static annual-balance ledgers net taxes vs services in a year; the PAYG dynamic — young contributors subsidizing the existing old — is a cross-cohort benefit they miss.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: PAYG programs are age-neutral to immigration
Contributions = future claims for every worker (false for unauthorized + early-mortality cohorts)
generators.theory_tested: Foreign-born (BPL>=150) AGE distribution in ipums_usa_borjas_panel should be younger across working ages than native, raising their PAYG contribution-years per claim-year.

generators.name: GE capital-adjustment wage restoration
generators.prompt: For any short-run native-wage-loss claim, require the long-run general-equilibrium counterpart: immigration raises the return to capital → induces investment → capital stock adjusts → the aggregate native wage effect returns toward zero (one-sector neoclassical). Tag every wage number SR (capital fixed) or LR (capital adjusted); never compare across the tag.
generators.retrodiction: Would have reconciled Borjas's short-run low-skill wage hit with the near-zero-to-positive long-run aggregate effect once capital adjusts (Ottaviano-Peri) — structurally the same omitted-capital-response mechanism as the capital-tax flip (G-LIF-A01), applied to wages instead of taxes.
generators.negative_space: Wage-shock lenses (B02–B06) mostly hold capital fixed; the capital-deepening response that restores wages is the missing GE step.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: Capital stock fixed when labor supply rises
generators.theory_tested: If capital adjusts, the cross-decade native <HS wage path should NOT track the immigrant-share rise 1:1 in the panel; the deviation is the capital response.

generators.name: Positive selection on unobservables
generators.prompt: Within an education×origin cell, do not treat the immigrant as the cell-average native: migrants are positively selected on unobservables (health, drive, risk-tolerance) — the migration decision is itself a filter. Apply a selection wedge before importing native cell-average welfare-use / earnings onto immigrants.
generators.retrodiction: Would have explained immigrant earnings catch-up and below-predicted welfare take-up: within-cell positive selection (the 'healthy migrant effect' + ambition filter) means the average same-education native overstates immigrant transfer use and understates immigrant earnings growth.
generators.negative_space: Fiscal ledgers apply native cell-average behavior to immigrants; the selection wedge (immigrant ≠ average same-education native) is unmodeled.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: An immigrant in a cell behaves like the average native in that cell
generators.theory_tested: Immigrant employment_rate and welfare-use in matched education cells should differ from native same-cell in the pro-immigrant direction, beyond what education alone predicts.

generators.name: Fiscal externality ≠ exclusion (welfare-economics framing)
generators.prompt: A negative net fiscal transfer is a TRANSFER (a financed externality), not a deadweight loss. Before treating −$X NPV as a reason to exclude, ask the first-best welfare-economics question: is the right response to TAX the surplus the migrant generates (Pareto-improving) rather than EXCLUDE the person (which forgoes the surplus)? Separate 'who pays' from 'is there a net gain to allocate'.
generators.retrodiction: Would have reframed the −$150k <HS lifetime NPV: if the migrant adds aggregate surplus (the immigration surplus is positive even in Borjas) AND a fiscal cost, the cost is a financing/redistribution problem, not a net loss — exclusion forgoes the surplus to avoid the transfer. The sign of the surplus and the sign of the fiscal balance are separable.
generators.negative_space: Cost ledgers treat the fiscal deficit as terminal harm; the welfare-economics distinction between a transfer-to-be-financed and a deadweight loss is absent.
unnamed_assumptions_surfaced: Net fiscal cost = net welfare loss
Exclusion is the only policy lever for a fiscal externality
generators.theory_tested: A cell can be fiscal-negative and immigration-surplus-positive simultaneously; the two ledgers should not be assumed to share a sign.

theories_tested.theory: Falling mean education with rising total output is a composition artifact, not a welfare loss
theories_tested.prediction: Total weighted earnings rise 1980->2023 in the IPUMS panel while share-weighted mean education falls
theories_tested.duckdb_test: SELECT YEAR, sum(INCTOT*PERWT) FILTER(WHERE INCTOT<9999998) FROM md.ipums_usa_borjas_panel GROUP BY YEAR ORDER BY YEAR
theories_tested.falsifier: Total real output falls as immigrant share rises

theories_tested.theory: The incumbent-fiscal ledger omits the largest welfare term (migrant place premium)
theories_tested.prediction: Per-migrant earnings gain at the border dwarfs the per-migrant lifetime fiscal NPV
theories_tested.duckdb_test: compare border-selection earnings jump (G-LIF-G04 inputs) to life.npv_education_benchmarks magnitudes
theories_tested.falsifier: Place premium is small relative to the fiscal NPV for the same cell
